record_identity.title: Subsidiary and affiliate pathways expose limits of geographic model-access controls

summary: The Financial Times reported that OpenAI and Google supplied advanced AI-model access to Singapore-based subsidiaries linked to Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent, whose parent companies have been placed on a United States Department of Defense blacklist. The reported transactions were legal. OpenAI reportedly suspended Alibaba-affiliated API users after suspected model distillation, while Google stated that geographic restrictions alone are insufficient because sophisticated actors can circumvent them. The report does not establish an export-control violation, data leakage, or use by a prohibited end user.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: A sovereign, regulatory, or security access boundary cannot be assessed solely by the geographic location of a user, tenant, subsidiary, reseller, or cloud endpoint. Beneficial ownership, organizational control, affiliate relationships, intermediary access, delegated credentials, and model-extraction behaviour may make a formally compliant regional boundary operationally porous. This observation therefore supports the existing Sovereign Assurance Boundary Porosity record while preserving the distinction between legal access, policy eligibility, entity control, end use, and prohibited circumvention.

source_records.source_context: The Financial Times reported that OpenAI and Google supplied model access to Singapore-based subsidiaries linked to Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent. It stated that the transactions were legal, that OpenAI later suspended Alibaba-affiliated users over suspected distillation, and that Google considered geographic restrictions insufficient against sophisticated circumvention.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary reporting for possible entity-control, affiliate, intermediary, and geographic-boundary porosity in advanced model access governance.

source_records.source_context: The report restated that OpenAI and Google confirmed supplying advanced model services to Singapore-based subsidiaries of Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent and described the companies' compliance and monitoring explanations.
source_records.relevance_note: Secondary corroborating report. It appears substantially derived from the Financial Times and is not treated as independent proof of the underlying transactions or legal status.

jurisdictional_context.sector: frontier AI platforms / cloud model services / cross-border technology access

possible_taxonomy_mapping.candidate_failure_description: An access-control regime relies primarily on geography, tenant location, or direct contracting entity while beneficial ownership, organizational control, affiliates, subsidiaries, intermediaries, or delegated access pathways permit materially equivalent access.
possible_taxonomy_mapping.classification_limit: The reports state that the transactions were legal and do not establish an export-control violation, prohibited end use, data leakage, military use, or a failed provider control. The observation preserves a governance-boundary risk and access-control design issue only.

related_or_similar_instruments: CAM-BS2026-AEON-012-PLATINUM — Annex K: Security Boundary Conditions & Runtime Interface
CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 — Appendix B: Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy
cam_internal.governance_layer: sovereign assurance boundary / entity and affiliate access control / cross-border model service governance / model-extraction monitoring
routing_note: Treat the Financial Times report as fresh evidence relevant to FM-0024 rather than as proof of a legal or compliance breach.
Preserve separate axes for geography, contracting entity, beneficial ownership, organizational control, affiliate status, intermediary access, end use, and extraction behaviour.
Do not infer prohibited military use, data leakage, or export-control violation from the current reporting.
cam_internal.validator_or_automation_impact: generated observation and master registry indexes require rebuild

observation_status.resolution_status: OpenAI reportedly suspended Alibaba-affiliated users for suspected distillation; broader entity-control and policy questions remain open
observation_status.observed_pattern: Geographic service availability and direct-customer controls may not fully capture corporate affiliation, ownership, intermediary, and end-use relationships.
observation_status.repair_linkage: FM-0024, PROP-0011, and PATCH-0012 already establish a sovereign-assurance-boundary framework; this observation extends the evidence beyond FedRAMP service continuity into entity and access-path porosity.
observation_status.monitoring_note: Seek provider statements, contractual eligibility rules, ownership-control tests, regulatory responses, and evidence distinguishing lawful access from policy circumvention or prohibited end use.

next_action: Add this observation to FM-0024 evidence review and assess whether the failure mode's scope should expressly include geographic eligibility, beneficial ownership, organizational control, affiliate and subsidiary status, reseller or intermediary access, delegated credentials, and model-extraction indicators. Do not amend Caelestis taxonomy in this VIGIL pass.
